title: Spag Bol
commentary: One of our quick and easy go to meals. The carrots are the secret to making the sauce sweet and tasty.
instructions: In a large pot, heat some oil over medium heat. Add the onion, and garlic, and sauté until softened.
Add the minced beef and cook until browned.
Add the grated carrots and cook for a few more minutes.
Stir in the tomato puree and simmer for 10 minutes. Add salt to season.
Meanwhile, cook the spaghetti according to package instructions.
Serve the sauce over the spaghetti.
notes: Omit the minced beef for a vegetarian version.
Serve with grated cheese on top.
You can also use chopped or crushed tomatoes instead of tomato puree.

title: Chicken & Leek Pasties
commentary: The pastry from this recipe is amazing BTW. I used it to make beef wellingtons for some friends once and they were dreaming about the pastry months later. If there's anything you take from this recipe, it's the pastry!

Rough puff pastry: 150g chilled unsalted butter, cut into small cubes
Filling: 2-3 medium leeks (about 500g), trimmed and finely sliced
About 400g boned chicken thigh and breast meat (or leftover cooked chicken), cut into thick slices
1 tablespoon rapeseed or olive oil (if using fresh chicken)
Sea salt and freshly ground black pepper
To finish: 1 medium egg, lightly beaten with 1 teaspoon milk, for glazing

instructions: To make the pastry, mix the flour with the salt, then add the cubed butter and toss until the pieces are coated with flour. Add just enough iced water (8-10 tablespoons) to bring the mixture together into a fairly firm dough.

Shape the dough into a rectangle with your hands and, on a well-floured surface, roll it out in one direction, away from you, so you end up with a rectangle about 1cm thick. Fold the far third towards you, then fold the nearest third over that (rather like folding a business letter), so that you now have a rectangle made up of 3 equal layers. Give the pastry a quarter-turn, then repeat the rolling, folding and turning process 5 more times. Wrap the pastry in a wax wrap or baking paper and rest it in the fridge for about 30 minutes, or up to an hour.

To make the filling, melt the butter in a frying pan, add the leeks and thyme, and sweat gently for 5-10 minutes, until the leeks are very tender. Stir in the cream and cook gently for 4-5 minutes to reduce and thicken. Stir in the mustard, season well and leave to cool.
If using fresh chicken, season well and fry in the oil in a frying pan over a medium-high heat until evenly browned and cooked through, then leave to cool.

Roll out the pastry on a lightly floured work surface to about 3mm thick. Using a plate or a cake tin as a template, cut out four 20cm circles; you may have to gather up the trimmings, lay them on top of each other, and re-roll them to get your fourth circle.

Spoon the leek mixture on to one half of each circle and pile the chicken on top. Brush the pastry edges with a little water, fold the other half of the pastry over the filling to form a half-moon shape and crimp well to seal.

Place on a lightly oiled baking sheet and brush the tops of the pasties with beaten egg. Bake in an oven preheated to 190°C/Gas Mark 5 for about 25 minutes, until the pastry is golden brown. Eat hot or cold.

title: Mini Meatloaves with Carrot Potato Mash
commentary: The girls really love these mini meatloaves, and they don't usually like meat otherwise.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C. Grease a 12-hole muffin tin (or loaf tin measuring about 25cm x 16cm). Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Bring a full kettle to the boil. Finely dice onion; grate carrot; peel and grate parsnip.
Heat a drizzle of oil in a large fry-pan on medium heat. Fry onion, carrot and parsnip about 4 minutes, until tender.
In a large bowl, combine mince, egg, breadcrumbs, soy sauce, salt, parsley, half the tomato sauce and half the cheese. Season with pepper.

Add cooked vegetables to the bowl with mince mixture. Mix well, using clean hands. Press about 1/4 cup of mixture into the muffin tins. Pack mixture into muffin or loaf tin tightly, so it stays together once baked. Top with remaining tomato sauce and cheese. Bake for 20 minutes (muffin tins) or 30 minutes (loaf tin).

While meatloaves cook, cut potatoes and carrot into 2cm pieces. Cook in pot of boiling water, for about 12-15 minutes, until very soft. Mash with butter, milk, chives and salt, until smooth. Cover, to keep warm.
Set cooked meatloaves aside, to rest for 5 minutes.
notes: Serve with a side of steamed vegetables such as broccoli for a complete meal.
Alternatively just do normal mash potatoes with a bit of wholegrain mustard.
attribution: Adapted from Dinner Done Better by My Food Bag

title: Chicken Chroizo Paella
commentary: One of our top recipes that we cooked when living in the UK. We loved using good quality spanish chorizo.
instructions: Remove the stalks and cores from the peppers and cut into thin slices. Peel and grate the garlic, finely chop the parsley leaves, and quarter the cherry tomatoes. Chop the chicken thighs into bite-sized chunks. Dissolve the chicken stock pot in 600ml boiling water. Pull the rosemary leaves from their sprigs.

Heat a splash of olive oil in a large frying pan on high heat. Season the chicken with salt and pepper. Add the chicken to the pan and fry for a few minutes until browned on all sides. Remove and set aside.
Add another splash of olive oil to the pan. Add the peppers and tomatoes. Cook for a few minutes until softened. Add the garlic, rosemary leaves, and chorizo. Cook for 3 minutes.
Add the rice and half of the parsley. Cook for a few minutes until the rice absorbs the oil.
Add the chicken stock and browned chicken. Stir well. Reduce the heat to medium-low and cook for 15-20 minutes. Do not stir while cooking to allow a crust to form on the bottom.
Once the liquid is absorbed, remove the pan from the heat. Cover with a clean tea towel and let rest for 5 minutes. Serve garnished with the remaining parsley and lemon wedges.

title: Chicken Katsu Curry
commentary: They used to have a Wagamama in Wellington, and I loved going there for the katsu curry. It turns out they still have them in the UK, and so we often went there for katsu curry too!

For the sauce (from scratch): 2.5cm piece of ginger, peeled and grated

Make the sauce: Heat 2-3 tbsp vegetable oil in a saucepan. Add the onion, garlic, and ginger and cook until softened.
Add the curry powder and turmeric, and cook for another minute on low to medium heat.
Stir in 1 tbsp flour and cook for 1 minute with the spices.
Gradually add the chicken or vegetable stock, then the coconut milk. Add a pinch of sugar, salt, and a dash of soy sauce. Simmer until thickened.
Strain the sauce through a sieve, a little at a time, to make it smooth. Set aside and keep warm.
Make the dish: Cook the rice according to the packet instructions.
Slice each chicken breast in half horizontally to make 4 fillets. Coat each fillet in flour, dip in beaten egg, then coat in panko breadcrumbs.
Heat the oil in a frying pan over medium heat. Fry the chicken fillets until golden brown and cooked through. Drain on paper towels.
Slice the chicken into strips. Serve with rice, salad leaves, and plenty of curry sauce.

notes: For extra greens, serve with wok-fried vegetables or steamed broccoli.

title: Spaghetti Meatballs
commentary: Another easy spaghetti recipe.
instructions: Place the beef mince, grated onion, egg, breadcrumbs, dried mixed herbs, and salt in a large bowl. Mix well until all ingredients are combined.
Shape the mixture into small meatballs, about the size of a golf ball. I use a tablespoon scoop to measure and shape them evenly.

Heat a large frying pan over medium heat and add a little oil. Cook the meatballs in batches, turning occasionally, until browned on all sides and cooked through (about 10-12 minutes). Remove the meatballs from the pan and set aside.

In the same pan, add the minced garlic and cook for 1 minute until fragrant. Add the canned crushed tomatoes and bring to a simmer. Let it cook for about 10 minutes to thicken slightly. Add salt to season.
While the sauce is simmering, cook the spaghetti according to the package instructions until al dente. Drain and set aside, saving a little pasta water.
Return the meatballs to the sauce and simmer for another 5 minutes to let the flavors meld together, adding a splash of pasta water if needed to loosen the sauce.
Serve the meatballs and sauce over the cooked spaghetti. Sprinkle with grated Parmesan cheese before serving.
notes: Pork mince (or a pork-beef mix) also works well in this recipe.
For extra vegetables, add a grated carrot or courgette to the sauce.
If available, fresh herbs work well in the meatball mixture instead of dried.
Add BBQ sauce (or even better Feijoa BBQ sauce) to the meatball mix for more flavour.

title: Spaghetti Carbonara (Traditional)
commentary: Kimmie likes creamy pasta dishes. I really like this more traditional version of carbonara, which is creamy without using cream.

Main: 175g guanciale (or pancetta or block bacon), skin removed
100g parmigiano reggiano, finely shredded (or pecorino romano, or parmesan)

instructions: Cut guanciale into 0.5cm thick slices, then into batons.
Place eggs and yolks in a large bowl. Whisk to combine, then stir in parmesan and pepper.
Bring 4 litres of water to the boil with the salt. Add pasta and cook per packet directions.
Just before draining, scoop out 1 cup of pasta cooking water, then drain pasta.
While pasta is cooking, place guanciale in a non-stick pan over medium-high heat. Cook for 4-5 minutes until golden. No oil needed. If using garlic, add in the last minute.
Tip hot pasta into the pan and toss to coat in guanciale fat.

Transfer pasta and any residual fat in the pan into the bowl with the egg. Add 1/2 cup pasta cooking water. Stir vigorously for 1 minute until sauce transforms from watery to creamy and clings to pasta.
Transfer into warm bowls. Serve immediately, garnished with extra parmigiano reggiano, black pepper, and parsley if desired.
notes: Use bacon instead of guanciale and parmesan instead of parmigiano reggiano for a more economical version.

title: Creamy Mushroom Pasta
commentary: Kimmie loved creamy pasta meals!
ingredients: 1 tbsp chives, parsley, or thyme, chopped
instructions: Cook the pasta according to package instructions. Drain and set aside, reserving 1 cup of pasta water.
In a large pan, melt the butter over medium heat. Add the garlic and sauté for 1-2 minutes until fragrant.
Add the sliced mushrooms and cook until they release their moisture and become golden brown, about 5-7 minutes, then set aside on a plate.
Add the white wine into the pan and let it simmer until reduced by half.
Reduce heat to low and stir in the cream. Let it simmer for a few minutes until it thickens slightly.
Stir in the grated Parmesan cheese until melted and combined.
Return mushrooms and pasta to the pan, then add 1/3 cup of reserved pasta water. If the sauce is too thick, add more reserved pasta water a little at a time until desired consistency is reached.
notes: Replace the wine with vegetable stock if not using.
Worcestershire sauce can be added for extra flavour.

title: King Prawn Linguine
ingredients: 1/2 mild red chilli, deseeded and finely chopped
3 tbsp fresh flat leaf parsley, finely chopped
instructions: Put a large saucepan of water iwth 1 tbsp of salt over a high heat and bring to the boil for the pasta. Prep the onion, garlic, sub-dried tomatoes, and chilli. Pick the parseley leaves from their stalks and finely chop both (keeping them separate). Lastly, cut the prans in half lengthways.

Heat 1 1/2 tbsp of oil in a frying pay over a medium heat. One hot, add the onion, garlic, sun-dried tomatoes, and as much chilli as you dare. Season with 1/4 tsp salt and black pepper. Cook until the onion is soft, about 5 mins.
Add the linguine to your pan of boiling water. Cook until 'al dente', about 11 minutes (or following packet instructions). Once cooked, drain the linguine in a colander.

Add the red wine vinegar, the tin of chopped tomatoes, the passata, parsley stalks, and sugar to the fryign pan. Let the mixture simmer over a medium-low heat until you have a nice thick sauce, about 10 minutes.
Once teh sauce has thickened, stir in the king prawns. Cook for 2-3 minutes until the prawns are pink on the outside and opaque all the way through.
Combine the pasta and sauce. Sprinkle over most of the parsley leaves and toss everything togetehr to mix thoroughly. Serve sprinkled with the remaining parsley leaves.
notes: We often just use frozen prawns for this recipe.
attribution: Recipes That Work by Hello Fresh

title: Baked Mac and Cheese

Sauce: 3 cups milk, warmed (low or full fat)
1 cup freshly shredded mozzarella (or more cheese of choice)

instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C.
Cook pasta: Bring a large pot of salted water to the boil. Add macaroni and cook per packet directions, but reduce cooking time by 1 minute (cook 'al dente'). Drain well.

Toss in butter: Return drained pasta to the pot, add 1 tbsp butter and toss until melted and coating each piece. Set aside while you make the sauce (this helps stop the pasta swelling and becoming stodgy).
Make the topping: Combine panko breadcrumbs, melted butter and 1/4 tsp salt in a small bowl. Set aside.
Make the sauce (roux method): In a large saucepan or ovenproof skillet, melt 4 tbsp butter over medium heat. Add flour and cook, stirring constantly, for 1 minute to make a roux.
Add milk: Add about 1 cup of warmed milk, whisk to dissolve the roux, then add remaining milk and whisk until smooth and lump-free.
Season and thicken: Add salt and any optional seasonings. Cook, stirring frequently, for 5-8 minutes until the sauce thickens to a cream consistency (it should coat the back of a spoon).
Add cheese: Remove the sauce from the heat and stir in the shredded cheeses - they don't need to fully melt on the stove. Taste and adjust salt.

Assemble: Pour the cheese sauce into the pot with the butter-coated macaroni and stir quickly to combine. Transfer the mixture to a baking dish or keep in an ovenproof skillet. Sprinkle the breadcrumb topping evenly over the surface.
Bake: Bake at 180°C for about 25 minutes, or until the top is light golden and the sauce is bubbly. Do not overbake or the dish will dry out.
Serve: Remove from oven and serve immediately, garnished with chopped parsley if desired.
notes: Grate your own cheese for best results — pre-grated cheese often contains anti-caking agents that can make sauces grainy.
Tossing the cooked pasta with butter before adding sauce prevents the pasta from swelling and keeps the final dish saucy rather than stodgy.
This recipe reheats well; reheat gently and consider adding a splash of milk if the sauce has thickened in the fridge.
Make-ahead: Assemble without the breadcrumb topping, refrigerate, add breadcrumbs just before baking and bake until hot and golden.

title: Pesto Pasta
commentary: A really basic recipe for when you need something the kids will eat and can't be bothered to do anything else!
ingredients: 400 pasta of choice (ziti, penne or spaghetti)
instructions: Bring a large pot of water to the boil with the salt.
Add pasta and cook for the time indicated on the packet.
Just before draining, scoop out 1 cup of the pasta cooking water.
Drain pasta in a colander and let sit for a minute.
Transfer pasta to a bowl.
Add pesto and 1/4 cup of pasta water. Toss to coat pasta, adding more water if needed to make it silky and saucy, rather than dry and sticky.
Taste and add more salt and pepper if desired.
Serve immediately, garnished with fresh Parmesan.
notes: Make this with homemade pesto for the best flavour
The pasta cooking water is key - it emulsifies with the oil in the pesto to cling to every strand of pasta

title: Pineapple Fried Rice
commentary: This fried rice was a hit with the girls, especially the pineapple!

Fried rice: 200g shelled prawns (defrosted), or thinly sliced chicken, or cubed tofu, or 3-4 eggs
2-3 tablespoons oil (canola, peanut, sunflower or rice bran)
1 cup defrosted frozen peas or corn (or sliced green beans)
2 kaffir lime leaves or 1 small lime/lemon tree leaf, very finely sliced (optional)
1 x 400g can pineapple chunks, well drained (reserve juice for other use)
1/2 cup roasted chopped cashew nuts or peanuts
Fried rice sauce: 2 tablespoons fish sauce or soy sauce (or 1 tablespoon each)

instructions: Pat prawns/chicken/tofu dry and season with salt. Heat a drizzle of oil in a wok or large frypan over medium-high heat. Stir-fry protein for 4-5 minutes until cooked and golden. If using eggs, whisk with a pinch of salt and cook as an omelette, then break into pieces. Transfer protein/eggs to a large bowl and set aside.

Add another 2 tablespoons oil to the wok and stir-fry onion, carrot, garlic, cabbage and peas/corn/green beans for about 5 minutes until starting to caramelise. Transfer vegetables to the bowl with the cooked protein.
Mix all fried rice sauce ingredients together in a small bowl.
Add a drizzle more oil to the pan, add turmeric, ground coriander, curry powder, kaffir lime leaf and lemongrass (if using) and sizzle for 30 seconds.

Add cooked rice, drained pineapple, chopped cashews/peanuts and the fried rice sauce. Stir-fry and toss for about 5 minutes until well combined and heated through, allowing some rice to brown and form little crusty bits if desired.
Return the cooked vegetables and protein/eggs to the pan, toss to combine, and season to taste with extra soy/fish sauce or salt.
Serve topped with crispy shallots, spring onion and chopped chilli if using.
attribution: Nadia's Comfort Kitchen by Nadia Lim

title: Chow Mein
commentary: Noodle comfort food.

Main: 200g chicken breast or thigh fillets, thinly sliced
1 1/2 tbsp peanut oil (or other cooking oil)
Chow Mein Sauce: 1 1/2 tbsp soy sauce, all purpose or light
1 1/2 tbsp Chinese cooking wine OR Mirin
2 tsp sugar (reduce to 1 tsp if using Mirin)

Sauce: Mix together cornflour and soy sauce, then mix in remaining ingredients.

Chicken & Noodles.Marinate Chicken: Pour 1 tbsp of Sauce over the chicken, mix to coat, set aside to marinate for 10 minutes.

Chicken & Noodles.Noodles: Prepare the noodles according to the packet instructions, then drain.

Cooking: Heat oil in wok or large fry pan over high heat.
Add garlic and stir fry for 10 seconds or until it starts to turn golden - don't let it burn!
Add chicken and stir fry until the surface gets a tinge of browning but inside is still raw - about 1 minute.
Add the cabbage, carrot, and the white pieces of shallots (i.e. from the base of the stalk). Stir fry for 1 1/2 minutes until the cabbage is mostly wilted.
Add the noodles, Sauce and water*. Stir fry for 1 minute, tossing constantly.
Add bean sprouts and remaining shallots/scallions. Toss through for 30 seconds or until the bean sprouts just start to wilt.
Remove from heat and serve immediately.

notes: Bacon works well in this too. Or just pork mince.
I tend to use the dry egg noodles that you boil for a bit first.

title: Thai Green Curry
commentary: Maybe not so authentic, but it's quick easy and yummy.
ingredients: 400g chicken breast fillets, sliced into thin strips
1 can baby corn, drained and halved
instructions: Cook rice according to package instructions.
Meanwhile, heat a large frying pan or wok over medium-high heat. Add a little oil and cook the sliced onion for 2-3 minutes until softened.
Add the chicken strips and cook for 4-5 minutes until browned all over.
Add the green curry paste and cook, stirring, for 1 minute until fragrant.
Pour in the coconut milk and bring to a simmer.
Add the bamboo shoots, capsicum, carrot, baby corn, and green beans. Simmer for 8-10 minutes until the vegetables are tender and the chicken is cooked through.
Stir in the fish sauce, brown sugar, and lemon or lime juice. Adjust seasoning to taste.
Serve the curry over the cooked rice, garnished with fresh coriander leaves.
notes: The key to this recipe is to balance the flavours of salty (fish sauce), sweet (brown sugar), sour (lime juice), and spicy (curry paste). Adjust to taste as needed.
You can substitute the vegetables with what you have on hand such as zucchini, brocolli, cauliflower, or snow peas.
If you don't have green curry paste, you can make your own using chilli powder, tumeric, ground corriander, and ground cumin.
Keffir lime leaves would go very well in this recipe!
attribution: Adapted from what Nan used to make

title: Butter Chicken Curry
commentary: A standard dish which the kids like. I've been making variantions of this for years but the recipe has evolved over time.
ingredients: 500g chicken (thigh, but breast also works), 2cm cubed
instructions: Cook rice according to packet instructions.
Heat a frying pan on medium-high heat and add a drizzle of oil. Add onion and cook until translucent.
Add chicken and cook until browned on all sides.
Add spices, and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add tomato puree, and bring to a simmer for 10 minutes.
Add butter and cream and then cook for a further 2 minutes until butter is melted and sauce is heated through.
Serve with rice and naan bread.
notes: To make this a little more healthy, feel free to add vegetables and reduce the cream!
To make this a little less healthy, add about 3 tbsp of sugar to the curry sauce for that real NZ mall butter chicken flavour.
This recipe also works if youi substitute the cream for 1 can of coconut cream and/or the chicken for paneer.

title: Palak Paneer Curry
commentary: This isn't a traditional palak paneer recipe, but it's what the kids like! The grated carrots help to bulk it out and sweeten the sauce.
ingredients: 1 tin (400ml) coconut cream (or use 1 cup cream)
instructions: Cook the basmati rice according to the package instructions.
Cut the paneer into 1.5cm cubes.
Heat oil in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add the paneer cubes and cook, turning occasionally, until golden brown on all sides, about 5-7 minutes. Remove from the pan and set aside.
In the same pan, add more oil if needed, then cook the diced onion and grated carrots until onion is softened and translucent, about 5 minutes.
Add the minced garlic and ginger, and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Stir in the garam masala and turmeric, and cook for 1 minute.
Add the chopped tomatoes and coconut cream. Stir to combine and bring to a simmer. Cook for 10 minutes, allowing the sauce to thicken slightly.
Add the baby spinach leaves and cook until wilted, about 2-3 minutes.
Blend with an immersion blender until smooth (optional). Season with salt to taste.
Return the paneer to the pan and simmer for another 5 minutes to heat through.
Serve the palak paneer curry over the cooked basmati rice with naan bread on the side.
notes: For a non-vegetarian option you could use chicken thigh instead of paneer.

title: Sausage Rolls
commentary: Everybody likes sausage rolls! These ones work well cold too.
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper. Heat a drizzle of oil in a fry-pan on medium heat. Fry onion, carrot and apple for 3-4 minutes, until softened. Set aside to cool slightly.
While vegetables are cooking, in a large bowl combine sausage mince, breadcrumbs, egg, and salt. Add the cooked vegetables and use a wooden spoon to mix well.
Place pastry sheets on a flat surface, and cut in half with a sharp knife so you have 2 rectangles with short sides facing you (6 in total).

Divide sausage mixture into 6 even portions and mould a portion into a log shape on the bottom third of each pastry piece. Roll pastry and sausage mixture away from you to enclose tightly, with about 2cm overlap of pastry for the seam. Trim off any excess pastry and reserve. Place roll on prepared tray, seam-side down.
Brush sausage rolls with milk and bake for 25-30 minutes, until pastry is puffed and golden. Leave to cool on tray for at least 5 minutes before serving.
notes: Use any leftover pastry to make small scrolls. Marmite and cheese works well.
attribution: Adapted from Kiwi Favourites by My Food Bag

title: Mince and Macaroni
commentary: This was one of my favourite meals growing up. I liked it with lots of HP or tomato sauce. Nan liked it too as it was pretty easy to make!
instructions: In a largefrying pan, saute the onion, garlic, and chopped mushrooms.
Add the mince and brown it.
Add the mcaroni and stir, then add 3 cups of hot water, along with cinnamon and worcestershire sauce. Simmer gently for 10-15 minutes until macaroni is tender.
Meanwhile, mix the cornflour with water to form a paste.
Add cornflour to macaroni mixture, stir well and keep stirring until you bring to boil. Cook for 1-2 minutes (add extra water if too thick).
Season to taste.

title: Chicken Curry Filos
commentary: When making this recipe we bought puff pastry instead by accident, but it worked just as well.
ingredients: 2 tbsp cornflout, mixed with 2 tbsp water
8-10 sheets filo pastry (can use puff pastry instead)
2-3 tbsp melted butter or oil, for brushing
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C. Line a baking tray with baking paper.

Heat oil in a large frying pan over high heat. Pat chicken dry and season with salt and pepper. Add to pan and cook for 2 minutes each side, until golden. Add onion and spices and cook for 3 minutes until onion is softened.

Reduce heat to medium and add coconut cream. Simmer for 3 minutes. Add cornflour mixture, courgette, and spinach to pan and cook for 2 minutes until veges are tender and sauce has thickened. Remove from heat, stir in soy sauce, and shred chicken in pan with two forks. Season to taste.

PPlace 1 sheet of filo on a dry, clean bench with short edge closest to you. Lightly brush with melted butter/oil and place another sheet on top. Brush again with melted butter/oil and fold in half lengthways. Brush folded sheet with melted butter or oil.

Spoon 1/5 of curry mixture in corner of filo. Fold over to form a triangle at the bottom of the sheet. Fold up to form a further triangle and continue folding until the whole sheet is wrapped around the filling. Repeat with remaining sheets and filling.
Place filos on prepared baking tray. Brush with melted butter/oil and sprinkle with sesame seeds if using. Bake for 8-10 minutes until golden and crisp.
Serve with chutney if using.

title: Pistachio & Cranberry Stuffing Balls
commentary: It turns out one of our favourite parts of a roast chicken is the stuffing, so why not have a meal based on stuffing! The tofu keeps the stuffing balls nice and moist.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C (or 200°C fan bake).

Finely chop cranberries. Pat tofu dry and crumble into a medium bowl. Add cranberries, onion powder, garlic powder, italian herbs, lemon pepper, pistachios, flour, plant-based milk, breadcrumbs, and salt to bowl and mix well using your hands, until combined. Set aside.
Peel and dice the pumpkin into 1cm cubes. Toss pumpkin on a medium lined oven tray with oil and season with salt and pepper. Roast on lower rack for 25-30 minutes, until golden.
Squeeze tofu mixture firmly into golf ball-sized balls and place on a lined oven tray. Drizzle stuffing balls with oil. Bake on upper oven rack for 15-20 minutes, until golden and cooked through.

Trim beans and asparagus. Heat oil in a medium frypan on high heat. Cook beans and asparagus with a pinch of salt for 4-5 minutes, until tender and starting to brown. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
Just before serving, heat gravy in a small pot on medium heat for 2-3 minutes to warm through.
Serve roasties, beans and stuffing balls topped with gravy. Serve horseradish aioli on the side for dipping.
notes: Use your hands to crumble the tofu. The pieces should be no larger than 1/2 cm.
attribution: My Food Bag: Dinner Done Well

title: Sushi Bowls
instructions: In a bowl, mix the cooked sushi rice with sushi seasoning to taste.
Divide the seasoned rice into 4 bowls.
Top each bowl with avocado slices, cucumber, carrot, smoked salmon, edamame beans, and nori strips.
Drizzle with Kewpie mayo and serve immediately.
notes: You can make your own sushi seasoning, but I've never had much luck with it.

title: Vegeful Frittata
commentary: I didn't think that I liked frittatas until I tried this recipe. It's great, and since then I've started ordering the frittata on our Qantas flights between Sydney and Wellington! Lexie and Kaira love this one too.
ingredients: 1/2 cup sour cream, cream, or creme fraiche
2 corn cobs, kernels removed (or 1 1/2 cups canned or defrosted frozen corn kernels)
2-3 cups diced roast pumpkin, kumara, or potato
2 cups chopped asparagus, green beans, or broccoli
tomato relish or chutney, to serve
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C.
In a large bowl, whisk together the eggs and sour cream until smooth. Stir in the feta and thyme. Season with salt and pepper.

Heat a drizzle of oil in a large ovenproof frypan over medium heat. Add the spring onions and corn kernels and cook for 1-2 minutes until softened. Remove from heat and toss through remaining vegetables.
Pour the egg mixture over the vegetables in the pan. Sprinkle with grated cheese. Bake for about 20 minutes until frittata is set.
Remove from oven and let sit for 5 minutes before slicing. Serve with tomato relish or chutney.
notes: You can swap or add any vegetables you have on hand. Great for using up leftovers.

title: Gnocchi
commentary: I really love this recipe. I've made it a couple of times for my parents and it's never failed to impress.

Gnocchi: 700-800g agria potatoes, scrubbed (leave the skin on the potatoes)

instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C. Place whole potatoes on an oven tray and bake for about 1 hour (depending on the size of potatoes) until very tender - test with a skewer or tip of a sharp knife; it should poke right through with no resistance.

Cut potatoes in half and peel off the skins while still warm. Push warm potato flesh through a potato ricer or a sieve (with the help of the back of a large spoon) into a large bowl. This ensures light gnocchi; whatever you do, do not mash the potato.

Add egg and salt and mix in with a fork. Sift in flour and use your hands to bring together as a soft dough. Add a little more flour if needed - it should feel dry to the touch, but not crumbly. Knead a few times, then divide into six pieces.

On a lightly floured surface, roll each piece out into a log about 30cm long, then cut into bite-sized 2cm pieces. Gently push the tip of your thumb or finger into the centre of each piece to make a little indent (this makes the perfect little hollow for sauce to pool in). Place in a large dish or on a tray (with a lip) dusted with flour, and shake pieces around to coat. Set aside until ready to cook.

Bring a large pot of well-salted water to the boil and preheat oven to grill. While water is coming to the boil, make the sauce. Heat olive oil in a large heatproof fry pan on medium heat. Sizzle garlic for 1-2 minutes (but do not let it brown), then stir in crushed tomatoes. Simmer for 5-8 minutes until thickened, and season to taste with salt and pepper. Stir through basil.

Drop about half of the gnocchi (one by one) into the boiling water and cook for a couple of minutes, scooping them out with a slotted spoon as they bob to the surface. Transfer straight into the pan of hot tomato sauce.

Once all the gnocchi are in the pan, gently toss to coat in sauce and top with mozzarella and parmesan. Place under the oven grill for about 5 minutes or until sauce is bubbling and mozzarella is melted. Serve immediately.
notes: This recipe also works well with pumpkin or kumara instead of potato.
attribution: Adapted from a recipe by Nadia Lim

title: Hiroshima Okonomiyaki
commentary: We encountered Hiroshima-style okonomiyaki during our holiday to Japan. We love it and have included it here, but we don't make it that often as it is quite tricky and messy!
ingredients: 5g tororo konbu (shaved dried konbu, optional, see notes)
2 tbsp tenkasu (fried batter bits, see notes)
instructions: Heat a flat griddle pan or frypan to 170°C.
Mix flour and water in a bowl until lump free.
Oil the pan thinly and pour about ¾ of the batter onto the pan, spreading to a 20cm circle.
Turn heat up to 200°C. Spread bonito flakes and tororo konbu (if using) on the batter.
Pile cabbage and bean sprouts within the circle, about 8cm tall. Sprinkle a pinch of salt.
Press down vegetables slightly, add tenkasu and shallots/scallions, then spread pork slices over. Sprinkle salt & pepper.
Cook ~5 mins until edge of batter curves up. Do not move until this point.
Spread remaining batter over the pile to glue toppings together.
Slide spatula under, gently lift and turn over. Collect scattered vegetables and tidy up.
Cook 8-10 mins until cabbage is wilted and soft.
After 6 mins, stir fry noodles on unused area of pan or new frypan at 200°C for a couple of minutes. Shape noodles into a circle matching okonomiyaki diameter.
Lift okonomiyaki and place on top of noodles.
Reduce pan to 170°C. Clean and oil pan.
Crack an egg onto oiled area, break yolk and spread to a circle matching okonomiyaki.
While egg is half raw, lift okonomiyaki and place on egg.
After 10-20 sec, lift and turn over so egg is on top.
Spread okonomiyaki sauce over egg, sprinkle aonori.
Cut in half and serve hot off the pan or transfer to plates.
notes: Tororo konbu is optional; substitute with shio-konbu or increase bonito flakes to 2 tbsp if unavailable.
Tenkasu are fried bits from tempura; can be bought or collected when making tempura.
Use ready-to-use, boiled fresh, or boiled dried noodles. Cook to al dente or slightly harder.
Okonomiyaki sauce: Mix 1 tbsp Worcestershire sauce, 3 tbsp tomato ketchup, 1 tsp soy sauce, 1 tsp honey, 1/2 tsp dashi stock powder diluted in 1 tsp water (optional).
Use the back of a ladle to spread batter in a circle.
Cover toppings with pork slices to prevent scattering when flipping.
Use two spatulas if needed for flipping.
If noodles are sticky, add some water.

title: Pumpkin Soup
commentary: We like to make pumpkin soup. This recipe is really easy and quick.

Main: 3 cups vegetable or chicken broth/stock, low sodium
Finishes: 1/2 - 3/4 cup cream, half and half, or milk

instructions: Cut the pumpkin into 3cm slices. Cut the skin off and scrape seeds out. Cut into 4cm chunks.

Place pumpkin, onion, garlic, broth and water in a pot (liquid won't quite cover all the pumpkin). Bring to a boil, uncovered, then reduce heat and simmer rapidly until pumpkin is tender (about 10 minutes).
Remove from heat and use a stick blender to blend until smooth.
Season to taste with salt and pepper, stir through cream (never boil soup after adding cream, cream will split).
Ladle soup into bowls, drizzle over a bit of cream, sprinkle with pepper and parsley if desired. Serve with crusty bread!
notes: If you don't have cream, use milk or add a knob of butter.
We sometimes add sour cream when serving.

title: Chicken and Corn Soup
commentary: A classic comfort food for when we had the flu.

Stock: 2 chicken backs (carcases) or bones left over from cooked whole chicken
Place all ingredients in a large stock pot with lid and cover with water (about 2 litres).
Bring to boil over medium heat, skim well to remove scum. Reduce heat and simmer gently covered for 1 1/2 to 2 hours.
Strain into large jug. Pick meat off carcas and reserve.
Soup: Combine in a large saucepan the finely chopped onion and grated ginger with 1-2 tbsp oil and cook until soft but not browned.
Remove from heat and stir in 7-8 cups of chicken stock, creamed sweetcorn, crumbled stock cubes, and meat off chicken carcas. Return to stove top and heat gently on medium.

Meanwhile, in a small bowl mix 4-5 tbsp cornflour with 4-5 tbsp water to make a paste. Then stir this into the saucepan mixture with a wooden spoon. Bring to the boil, stirring with spoon and boil for 2-3 minutes as it thickens. Cool slightly and add salt and pepper to taste.

notes: The amount of cornflour will determine the thickness of the soup.
Can add 1/2 cup of frozen corn kernals.
I like to sometimes add a beaten egg and / or noodles to the soup for somehting extra.
Should it be celery instead of parsley in the stock?

title: Cottage Pie
commentary: A good hearty classic. I like to have it with HP sauce.
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C fan bake.
Peel and chop potatoes, add to a saucepan with water. Bring to the boil and reduce to medium heat to simmer for 15-20 mins until tender.
Heat the oil in a large frying pan, add onion and carrot, fry until softened.
Add the mince, garlic and herbs then fry until lightly browned. Stir in the red wine, Worcestershire sauce, tomato paste and stock. Simmer for 10 minutes.
Mix the cornflour with 2 tablespoons of water to make a slurry then add to the mince. Add the peas and stir until it heats up and thickens. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
Mash the potatoes with butter and milk and season to taste.
Divide the mince into 6 individual pie dishes and top with mashed potato, smoothing and then roughing up with a fork. Top with grated cheese.
Bake for 20 minutes or until the cheese is lightly browned with crispy bits.
notes: This recipe can also be made in one large pie dish.
Freeze in an airtight container for up to 3 months.
Swap the beef for lamb mince to make a shepherd's pie.

title: Mince and Cheese Pie
commentary: We used to have this kiwi classic growing up. It's also great to prep in advance and reheat later.

Pie filling: 2 teaspoons cornflour mixed with ¼ cup water

instructions: Heat 1 tablespoon of olive oil in a medium frying pan over a medium heat. Cook onions and carrot for 5 minutes until soft. Add garlic and mince and brown (about 5 minutes).

Add all remaining pie filling ingredients, except cornflour and water, stir to combine and simmer for 20 minutes. Stir through the cornflour slurry and cook for a further 2 or 3 minutes until the gravy thickens.

Roll out ⅔ of the pastry to fit a 20-23cm round pie dish. Line the bottom of the dish with the pastry, fill with the mince filling and top with the cheese slices. Roll out the remaining pastry to fit the top of the pie and place on top, sealing the edges with your fingers or a fork.
Prick holes in the pastry using a fork and brush with egg wash. Bake at 180°C/350°F for 40 minutes until the pastry is golden brown and cooked through.
Serve immediately and store leftovers in the fridge for 4 days.
notes: To make mini pies use the same quantities but roll out the pastry and cut rounds that will fit in muffin tins. Pies will only take about 30 minutes to bake.

title: Schnitzel
commentary: One of Kimmie's favourite meals is schnitzel. I don't tend to like making it as often the coating sticks to the pan and the cuts of meat aren't great. But this recipe seams to be okay.

Pick one: 600g boneless pork (4 steaks / boneless chops, about 150g each)
600g veal or beef steaks (4 pieces, 150g each)
To serve: Horseradish sauce (if using beef or veal)

instructions: If using chicken breast, cut in half horizontally to create two steaks.

Place the protein of choice on a work surface and cover with baking paper or cling wrap. Use a rolling pin to pound to even thickness about 0.8 cm thick (don't pound too thin, hard to handle / might tear).
Sprinkle both sides of meat with salt and pepper.
Place flour in one dish, panko in another, and whisk eggs in a bowl.
Coat meat in flour, shake off excess. Dip into egg, shake off excess. Press into breadcrumbs, and turn, pressing to adhere, then transfer to plate. Repeat for all pieces.
Heat 1.5cm oil in a heavy based deep skillet over medium high heat.
Dip the end of a schnitzel in to check if the oil is hot enough - it should sizzle straight away.
Carefully place 2 schnitzels in the oil and cook for 3 minutes until deep golden, turn then cook the other side for 3 minutes.
Transfer to a paper towel lined plate to drain excess oil. Repeat with remaining schnitzel.
Serve with lemon wedges. Optionally serve with mushroom gravy or potato rosti.
notes: This recipe also works well with chips and a green salad.
Careful with how you dispose of the oil. Don't pour it down the sink!

title: Lamb Koftas
commentary: A delicious summery BBQ meal that we like.

Koftas: 1 tsp dried mixed herbs (or fresh finely chopped)
To Serve: 4 flat breads (use the flatbread recipe if you like)

instructions: Add all of the ingredients for the lamb koftas to a large bowl and mix to combine.
With damp hands, divide the meat into 8 equal portions and shape onto sausage shapes.
Start BBQing the vegetables first, turning occasionally. Then BBQ the koftas for 15 minutes or until cooked through. Turning to brown on each side every few minutes.
Meanwhile, mix the mint through the Greek yoghurt to make a quick mint sauce.
Just before serving, warm the flatbreads on the BBQ for about 30 seconds each.
notes: You can use skewers, but I tend not to.
Instead of the grilled vegetables, a hummus, tomato, and feta combo also works well.
Pearl couscous salad goes well inside these too, but it can get messy!

title: Pistachio & Cranberry Stuffing Balls
commentary: It turns out one of our favourite parts of a roast chicken is the stuffing, so why not have a meal based on stuffing! The tofu keeps the stuffing balls nice and moist.
instructions: Preheat oven to 220°C (or 200°C fan bake).

Finely chop cranberries. Pat tofu dry and crumble into a medium bowl. Add cranberries, onion powder, garlic powder, italian herbs, lemon pepper, pistachios, flour, plant-based milk, breadcrumbs, and salt to bowl and mix well using your hands, until combined. Set aside.
Peel and dice the pumpkin into 1cm cubes. Toss pumpkin on a medium lined oven tray with oil and season with salt and pepper. Roast on lower rack for 25-30 minutes, until golden.
Squeeze tofu mixture firmly into golf ball-sized balls and place on a lined oven tray. Drizzle stuffing balls with oil. Bake on upper oven rack for 15-20 minutes, until golden and cooked through.

Trim beans and asparagus. Heat oil in a medium frypan on high heat. Cook beans and asparagus with a pinch of salt for 4-5 minutes, until tender and starting to brown. Season to taste with salt and pepper.
Just before serving, heat gravy in a small pot on medium heat for 2-3 minutes to warm through.
Serve roasties, beans and stuffing balls topped with gravy. Serve horseradish aioli on the side for dipping.
notes: Use your hands to crumble the tofu. The pieces should be no larger than 1/2 cm.
attribution: My Food Bag: Dinner Done Well

title: Dumplings
commentary: I was taught how to make and fold dumplings by a friend who was taught by her parents as a kid. It's a useful life skill!
ingredients: Sweet chili sauce or soy sauce for serving
instructions: In a large bowl, combine the pork mince, cabbage, carrot, ginger, garlic, soy sauce, and sesame oil. Mix well.

Place a small spoonful of the filling onto the center of each dumpling wrapper. Moisten the edges with water, fold the wrapper over the filling, and pinch to seal pleating the edge in the process to make a half-moon.
Heat oil in large frying pan over medium heat. Add dumplings in batches, turn them twice so that they are golden brown on all sides.
Serve hot with sweet chili sauce or soy sauce to dip in.
notes: You can boil the dumplings instead, but I prefer frying them.
I also like to mix soy sauce and vinegar for dipping sauce.

title: Sweet Potato & Quinoa Patties
commentary: A nice dish with great flavour that the girls enjoyed.

Patties: 2 sweet potatoes, skin on, washed, and roughly chopped
1 fresh red chili, deseeded and finely chopped (optional)
Chreen tahini and lime dressing: 1/2 bunch coriander (save some leaves for serving)

instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C.

Arrange sweet potato pieces on a baking tray, drizzle with 1 tablespoon olive oil and bake for 20 minutes. At the same time put 750ml (3 cups) of water and the quinoa in a saucepan over a medium-high heat, bring it to the boil, then simmer for 15 minutes, or until the quinoa is cooked and all the liquid is gone.

When the spuds are cooked, pop them into a large mixing bowl, add your cooked quinoa and use a fork to mash everything together. Add the onion, garlic, cumin, and fresh chilli to the mix and season with salt and pepper. Shape the mixture into 8 large balls (you want these to look like little burger patties). Place the patties on a baking tray, drizzle with the remaining olive oil and bake for 15 minutes, or until they're golden.
To make the dressing, put all the ingredients into a blender, give it a whizz and job done!
When you patties are cooked, dollop some tahini dressing on each one, then garnish with some coriander and crushed walnuts.
notes: We substituted the tahini with peanut butter and it worked well.
Don't skimp on the dressing!
attribution: The Happy Cookbook by Lola Berry

title: Greek Lamb & Lentil Ragu
commentary: Greek food pulls off the clever trick of being both hearty and summery! This delicious dish of tomatoey lamb and lentils with nutty bulgur wheat and refreshing cucumber tzatziki will bring the holiday vibes straight to you.
ingredients: 2 onions, halved, peeled and thinly sliced
1 tsp fresh oregano leaves, roughly chopped
Salt and black pepper to taste
instructions: Toast the almonds in a frying pan over medium-high heat for 2-3 minutes until browned, then set aside.
Add oil to the pan and cook the lamb mince with salt and pepper until brown, about 5 minutes. Add onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft.
Add garlic, oregano, cumin and cinnamon sticks, cook for 1 minute.
Add lentils, chopped tomatoes and 1 chicken stock pot. Half fill the tomato tin with water and add to the pan. Simmer for 20 minutes until lentils are soft.
Meanwhile, boil water with remaining stock pot, add bulgur wheat, cover and remove from heat.
For tzatziki: deseed and grate cucumber, chop mint, mix with Greek yoghurt.
Fluff bulgur with fork, stir in olive oil and remaining mint. Serve with ragu, almonds and tzatziki (remove cinnamon sticks first).
notes: Remember to remove the cinnamon sticks before serving
attribution: Recipes That Work by Hello Fresh

title: Beef Burgers
commentary: The patties. It's all about the patties.
ingredients: 1 tbsp leafy herbs (parsley, coriander, thyme etc.)
burger toppings (lettuce, tomato, cheese, sliced beetroot, sauce etc.)
instructions: In a bowl, combine the beef mince, salt, breadcrumbs, and herbs. Mix well.
Form the mixture into 4 patties. Push down in the middle with your thumb to create a slight indentation (this will help the patties cook evenly).
Cook the patties on the BBQ or frying pan for 5 minutes on each side.
Toast the burger buns and assemble the burgers with your choice of toppings.
notes: Many burger recipes use eggs to keep the patties together, but I find them unnecessary.
I sometimes like to mix in mum's feijoa BBQ sauce to the patties, but the sugars in this does mean the patties can burn more easily.
This works best with beef mince that has fat in it (so not the premium mince). But get good mince from the butcher.
attribution: I dunno, it's a burger recipe.

title: Kumara, Chickpea, and Mushroom Burgers
commentary: We like to try vege burgers now and then. This recipe from Vegeful is one of the best.

Patties: 250g portobello mushrooms, stalks removed then diced
1/3 cup finely chopped basil, parsley, and/or coriander

instructions: Cook kumara in boiling salted water for about 10 minutes or until soft. Drain well, tiop back into pot and place over low heat for a few minutes to dry off.

Meanwhile, heat oil in a large fry pan on medium-high heat. Cook mushroomes and onion, with a good pinch of salt for 6-8 minutes or until soft and all moisture in the pan (from the mushrooms) has evaporated. Add another drizzle of oil, garlic, curry powder, cumin, and lemon zest and continue cooking for 1 minute.
Add mushroom and onion mixture to the pot with the kumara, along with chickpeas, breadcrumbs, egg, salt, and herbs. Use a potato masher to mash everything together and season with pepper.
Divide and roll mixture into 6 large balls (about 3/4 cup each). Flatten and shape each ball into patties 1.5cm thick. Place in the fridge to firm up while you prepare everything else.

Oil each patty and cook on BBQ hotplate or large frying pan over medium-high heat for a few minutes each side until golden and cooked through. If using, drizzle portobello mushrooms and eggplant slices with oil, season, and cook until soft and golden.
Top each patty with cheese and melt under the grill, if desired. Lightly toast burger buns.

To assemble burgers, spread bottom hald with relish, top with pickles, a patty and cheese, eggplant/mushroom (if using), tomato, onion, and lettuce. Mix mayo and curry powder together and spread on top half of bun.

title: Pork Satay Burgers with Paprika Sweet Potato Wedges
commentary: Another good burger recipe!
instructions: Pre-heat your oven to 220 degrees. Chop the sweet potatoes into wedges roughly the width and length of your index finger. Put them on a baking tray, drizzle over 2 tbsp of oil, sprinkle over half the paprika, ½ tsp of salt and a grind of pepper. Make sure everything is evenly coated and put in your oven to cook for 25 mins.

Roughly chop the coriander (stalks and leaves!) and zest the limes with a grater. Roughly chop the peanuts. Put the pork mince in a bowl and add three quarters of your coriander along with your lime zest, chopped peanuts, the other half of your paprika, half the soy sauce, ½ tsp of salt and a good grind of pepper.
Combine everything together with your hands and shape into four burgers about 2cm thick. Tip: Don't make them too thick, they will change shape as the proteins tighten up while they are being cooked.

Remove the tough rooty bit off the lettuce and cut into roughly 2cm slices. Peel, grate the carrot and mix it with the lettuce. Mix 2 tbsp of olive oil with the juice from one half of one of the limes and a pinch of salt and pepper, whisk together with a fork. Tip: If you're not that keen on lime, use a bit less than specified! Put most of your lettuce and carrot into a bowl (reserve a little to put on the burgers!) and throw in your limey dressing. Toss the salad.

Put a frying pan on medium heat and add 2 tbsp of oil. Add your burgers and cook for 5 mins on each side before adding them to a baking tray. Put the burgers in your oven to carry on cooking for 5 mins while you make the sauce.

Put the peanut butter in a bowl and add the juice from the other lime. Add 2 tbsp of water, the rest of the soy sauce and the rest of the coriander and mix everything together with a fork. Tip: Again, only add as much lime as you like to taste.

Turn your grill to high and cut the brioche buns in half. Tip: If your oven and grill aren't separate just move your sweet potato and burgers down a shelf so the top shelf is free for grilling! Put your buns under the grill for a minute or 2 on each side to toast them. Tip: Keep an eye on them, you don't want them to burn!

When everything is ready, spread your satay sauce on the bottom half of your buns, pop your burgers on top, top with some lettuce carrot mixture and pop the bun top on. Serve with a side of sweet potato wedges and salad!

title: Hot Dogs
commentary: Not much in the way or nutrition here, but it's quick and easy!
ingredients: tomato sauce and american mustard (to serve)
instructions: Cook the sausages on the frying pan until heated through.
Warm the buns if desired.
Place sausages in buns and top with sourkraut and sauces.
attribution: Does it really need any attribution?

title: Pita Nachos
commentary: We find a lot of the corn chips are too salty, so we prefer to make our own pita chips. They get super crispy.
ingredients: 1 packet pita bread, each cut into 8 triangles
500g beef mince, or brown lentils for vegetarian
1 tin black beans, drained and rinsed
2 tbsp taco seasoning (or mix 1 tsp each ground cumin, paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, dried oregano, and a pinch of cayenne pepper)
instructions: Preheat oven to 200°C (fan 180°C). Line a baking tray with baking paper.
Prepare the pita chips: lightly brush or spray pita triangles with oil, spread in a single layer on the tray and bake 8-12 minutes until crisp and golden. Remove and set aside.
Meanwhile heat a splash of oil in a large frying pan over medium heat. Add the diced onion and cook, stirring, for 2-3 minutes until softened and translucent.
Add the beef mince (or cooked brown lentils). Season with salt and pepper and cook, breaking up with a spoon, until browned and cooked through (about 6-8 minutes).

Stir in the tin of tomatoes, beans, and taco seasoning (or spice mix). Bring to a simmer, then reduce the heat and cook gently for 10-15 minutes until the sauce has reduced and thickened. Taste and adjust seasoning.
If using avocado and fresh tomato as toppings, prepare them now: dice tomato and mash the avocado; squeeze a little lemon or lime over the avocado to prevent browning.

To assemble: scatter the pita chips on a serving platter or divide between plates, spoon the mince mixture over the chips, then top with grated cheese, diced tomato, avocado and other toppings such as olives, sour cream or salsa.
notes: You can use regur corn chips (we prefer the mexicano natural ones), but we like the pita chips better.

title: Quesadillas
commentary: A simple dish which the kids like to eat, although they mostly just like the tortillas!

Quesadillas: 2 cups (200g) shredded Monterey Jack cheese (or other of choice)
1 cup corn kernels (frozen thawed or canned drained)
Filling of Choice, below (beef, chicken or vegetable)

Assembling Quesadillas: Place tortilla on work surface. Sprinkle one side with a bit of cheese, top with Filling of Choice. Sprinkle with corn, coriander and top with cheese. Fold in half.
Preheat non-stick skillet over medium-low heat (no oil; if pan is not non-stick, use 2 tsp oil).
Place quesadilla in skillet, press down lightly, cover with lid. Cook for 3 minutes until underside is super golden brown and crispy.
Carefully flip over the folded edge. Press down lightly. Cook for 3 minutes until crispy (no lid).
Transfer to cutting board, cut in half. Serve immediately.
Spice Mix: Mix all ingredients in a small bowl. Use for one of the Filling options below.
Beef Filling: Heat oil in a skillet over high heat. Add onion and garlic, cook for 2 minutes.
Add beef and cook, breaking it up as you go. Once it changes from pink to brown, add capsicum. Cook for 1 minute.
Add tomato paste, water and Spice Mix. Cook for 2 minutes. Transfer to bowl, cool.
Chicken Filling: Drizzle chicken with 1 tbsp oil, toss to coat. Sprinkle over Spice Mix, toss well to coat.
Heat 1 tbsp oil in a large skillet over high heat. Add onion and garlic, cook for 2 minutes. Add capsicum and cook for 1 minute. Transfer to bowl, cool.
Return pan to stove, reduce to medium heat. Add remaining ½ tbsp oil. Add chicken and cook for 3 minutes until deep golden. Turn and cook for 3 minutes until cooked through.
Transfer chicken to cutting board, rest for 2 minutes then dice and cool. Combine with capsicum mixture.
Vegetable Filling: Heat oil in a skillet over high heat. Add onion and garlic, cook for 2 minutes. Add capsicum, cook for 1 minute.
Add beans, corn, tomato paste, water and Spice Mix. Cook for 2 minutes. Transfer to bowl, cool.

notes: Serve with Sour cream, salsa, or guacamole

title: Burrito Bowl
commentary: Anything goes. This is a nice easy and adaptable meal.
ingredients: 1 can black beans, drained and rinsed
1 tbsp mexican spice mix (or chili powder, cumin, paprika, oregano)
instructions: Cook rice according to package instructions.
Heat drizzle of oil in a pan over medium heat. Add corn, cooking until tender (about 5-7 minutes).
Transfer corn to a bowl and add coriander, a pinch of salt, pepper, and lime juice. Mix well and set aside.
In the same pan, add a bit more oil if needed and sauté onion and red pepper until softened (about 5-7 minutes). Add garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Add crushed tomatoes and spices, stir, then add black beans to the pan and cook until heated through (about 3-5 minutes). Season with salt and pepper to taste.

To serve, divide rice between bowls. Top with the tomato and bean mixture, corn, avocado slices, cherry tomatoes, shredded lettuce, coriander, cheese, a dollop of sour cream, and corn chips for crunch.
notes: You can add beef or chicken mince to the tomato and bean mixture if you want a non-vege option.
Feel free to change the toppings and use whatever you have. Omit whatever isn't in season or is too expensive.
Mixing chipotle sauce into the sour cream works really well.
Don't forget the corn chips. The Mexicano brand ones are the best!

title: Enchiladas
commentary: A simple creamy enchiladas recipe which I used to make a lot as a student.
ingredients: 1 cup shredded cheese (cheddar or mozzarella blend)
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C.
In a large skillet, heat a little oil over medium heat. Add the diced onion and cook until softened, about 3-4 minutes.
Add the minced garlic and cook for another minute until fragrant.
Add the diced chicken to the skillet and cook until browned on all sides, about 5-7 minutes.
Stir in the diced capsicum, crushed tomatoes, sour cream, and taco seasoning. Simmer for 10 minutes until the sauce thickens slightly.
Spoon a few tablespoons of the chicken mixture onto each tortilla, roll them up, and place them seam-side down in a baking dish.
Pour any remaining sauce over the top of the rolled tortillas and sprinkle with shredded cheese.
Bake in the preheated oven for 20-25 minutes, until the cheese is melted and bubbly.
Serve hot, garnished with fresh coriander.

title: Vietnamese Pork Tacos
commentary: This recipie is great because it's very quick and easy to throw together.
ingredients: garlic aioli or QP mayo, to serve
instructions: In a large frying pan, heat a drizzle of olive oil. When hot, add onion and carrot and cook until softened. Add the garlic and cook until fragrant, 1 minute.
Add the pork mince and cook until browned, breaking up with a wooden spoon, 4 minutes.
Remove from heat and stir through kecap manis and soy sauce.
Warm the tortillas, and then top with lettuce leaves, spoonfuls of the pork mixture, a drizzle of garlic aioli or mayo, and sprinkle with crispy fried shallots. Serve immediately.
notes: Instead of cooking the carrot, it also works well to use raw julienned carrot.

title: Mexican Shredded Beef (and Tacos)
commentary: We cooked this in our pressure cooker for Len and Armine, and it worked well. We even used the leftovers for a mexican pizza the next day.

Spice Mix: 1.5 tbsp chipotle powder (adjust spiciness to taste)
2 tsp onion powder or garlic powder (or 1 tsp of each)
Beef: 3 lb / 1.5kg beef chuck or brisket (or gravy or any other slow cooking beef), cut into 4 pieces
2 cups (500 ml) beef or chicken broth/stock

Cook: Combine the Spice Mix ingredients in a bowl. Sprinkle 4 teaspoons over the beef and pat so it sticks.
Heat the olive oil in a large heavy based pot over high heat. Add the beef (in batches if necessary) and brown well on all sides. Remove onto a plate.
Turn the stove down to medium. If the pot looks dry, add more olive oil.
Add the garlic and onion and cook for 3 minutes until soft.
Add the orange juice and lime juice, and scrape the bottom of the pot so the brown bits mix into the liquid.
Add tomato, beef stock, water and remaining spice mix. Mix, then return beef into pot.
Put the lid on, bring to a simmer then turn the stove down so it is bubbling gently, not rapidly.
Cook for 2 hours, then remove lid and simmer for another 30 minutes until beef is tender enough to shred.
Remove the beef from sauce, shred with 2 forks.
Leave the sauce to simmer with the lid off for 10 to 15 minutes to reduce and thicken to your taste. Adjust salt to taste. Optionally puree with stick blender to make it smooth.
Toss beef back into the sauce (can reserve some sauce for drizzling on tacos if you want, there's plenty).
Transfer beef into large dish and serve.
Tacos: To make tacos, serve the beef with warmed small tortillas, avocado slices, Pico de Gallo, shredded cheese, sour cream, lime wedges and extra coriander leaves.

notes: Other cooking methods: slow cooker for 8 hours on low, pressure cooker & Instant Pot on high for 50 minutes. For Slow Cooker and Pressure Cooker - after deglazing the pot in step 5 with the orange juice and lime juice, pour the liquid and onions into the slow/pressure cooker (IP) and add all remaining ingredients + beef. Cook per above. Transfer the liquid to a saucepan to reduce the sauce on the stove, as required by the recipe. Oven - after completing step 6, cover and place in 160C oven for 3 - 3 1/2 hours or until the beef is tender enough to shred. The liquid should reduce enough to make a thick sauce. If not, just simmer it on the stove until thickened to your liking.

title: Pizzas
commentary: Now you might be thinking, why is this under the Sunday meals section? Well, sunday night is usually pizza night. It's an easy tasty meal to end the weekend with (plus we get leftovers for monday lunch!).

Bases: Toppings of choice (pepperoni, mushrooms, peppers, etc.)
Chicken & Plum Sauce: Plum sauce (cranberry or apricot sauce can be used as alternatives)

instructions: Preheat the oven to 220°C.
Roll out the pizza dough on a floured surface.
Spread the tomato puree evenly over the base.
Add mozzarella cheese and your choice of toppings.
Bake in the preheated oven for 12-15 minutes.
notes: We make our own pizza dough in the bread maker.
We make our own pizza sauce by mixing tomato puree with herbs such as oregano, basil, and/or garlic and a pinch of salt.
Our top combinations are tomato, ham, & pineapple, potato & rosemary, and chicken & plum sauce.

title: Sunday Roast
commentary: Growing up, we would have a roast every sunday. Even when I moved out, we would often host a roast night with friends.

Roast Lamb: Leg of lamb, seasoned with garlic and rosemary
Sides: Roasted vegetables (potatoes, kumara, pumpkin, yams, or parsnips)
Boiled vegetables (broccoli, carrots, peas, asparagus, or green beans)

instructions: I'm not going to provide a roast recipe, as there are better instructions elsewhere. I'm just going to list the trimmings and sides we usually have with a roast.

title: Steak and Chips
commentary: Lots of people don't know how to cook steak, and just chuck it on the BBQ with no seasoning. I cooked steak for some of my friends using this method some of my friends said it was the best steak they'd ever had.

Steak and Chips: 2 big scotch fillet steaks (aim for 125g per person)
1 bunch green vegetables (green beans, broccoli, asparagus, etc.)
Chimmichurri Sauce: 1/2 cup fresh parsley, chopped (or other leafy green herbs)
Plum Sauce: I use my mum's plum sauce.

instructions: Preheat the oven to 220°C (428°F).
Cut the potatoes into chips and soak them in water for 30 minutes.
Drain and pat the potatoes dry, then toss with oil and seasonings.
Spread the chips on a baking tray and bake for 30-40 minutes until golden and crispy.
Meanwhile, season the steaks with salt and pepper and cook them in a hot pan for 3-4 minutes each side for medium-rare.
Serve the steaks with the chips and your choice of sauce.
notes: The key to cooking steak is to firstly let it come to room temperature before cooking, secondly to season it with salt 5 minutes before cooking, and thirdly to let it rest for 5 minutes after cooking.
Easier than making chips, we often just roast chopped potatoes.
You can use other cuts of steak, but scotch fillet is my favourite.

title: Chicken & Leek Pasties
commentary: The pastry from this recipe is amazing BTW. I used it to make beef wellingtons for some friends once and they were dreaming about the pastry months later. If there's anything you take from this recipe, it's the pastry!

Rough puff pastry: 150g chilled unsalted butter, cut into small cubes
Filling: 2-3 medium leeks (about 500g), trimmed and finely sliced
About 400g boned chicken thigh and breast meat (or leftover cooked chicken), cut into thick slices
1 tablespoon rapeseed or olive oil (if using fresh chicken)
Sea salt and freshly ground black pepper
To finish: 1 medium egg, lightly beaten with 1 teaspoon milk, for glazing

instructions: To make the pastry, mix the flour with the salt, then add the cubed butter and toss until the pieces are coated with flour. Add just enough iced water (8-10 tablespoons) to bring the mixture together into a fairly firm dough.

Shape the dough into a rectangle with your hands and, on a well-floured surface, roll it out in one direction, away from you, so you end up with a rectangle about 1cm thick. Fold the far third towards you, then fold the nearest third over that (rather like folding a business letter), so that you now have a rectangle made up of 3 equal layers. Give the pastry a quarter-turn, then repeat the rolling, folding and turning process 5 more times. Wrap the pastry in a wax wrap or baking paper and rest it in the fridge for about 30 minutes, or up to an hour.

To make the filling, melt the butter in a frying pan, add the leeks and thyme, and sweat gently for 5-10 minutes, until the leeks are very tender. Stir in the cream and cook gently for 4-5 minutes to reduce and thicken. Stir in the mustard, season well and leave to cool.
If using fresh chicken, season well and fry in the oil in a frying pan over a medium-high heat until evenly browned and cooked through, then leave to cool.

Roll out the pastry on a lightly floured work surface to about 3mm thick. Using a plate or a cake tin as a template, cut out four 20cm circles; you may have to gather up the trimmings, lay them on top of each other, and re-roll them to get your fourth circle.

Spoon the leek mixture on to one half of each circle and pile the chicken on top. Brush the pastry edges with a little water, fold the other half of the pastry over the filling to form a half-moon shape and crimp well to seal.

Place on a lightly oiled baking sheet and brush the tops of the pasties with beaten egg. Bake in an oven preheated to 190°C/Gas Mark 5 for about 25 minutes, until the pastry is golden brown. Eat hot or cold.

title: Focaccia Bread
commentary: This is my go-to focaccia recipe. A couple of times as potluck lunches other people have made focaccia bread too, but this one is always the best.
ingredients: 1 tablespoon chopped fresh thyme or 1 teaspoon dried
1 tablespoon chopped fresh rosemary or 1 teaspoon dried
instructions: In a cold medium skillet, combine olive oil, minced garlic, thyme, rosemary, and the black pepper. Place the pan over low heat and cook, stirring occasionally, 5 to 10 minutes or until aromatic, but before the garlic browns. Set aside.

In a large bowl, combine the warm water, yeast, and honey. Stir a few times then let sit for 5 minutes. Add 1 cup of the flour and a 1/4 cup of the infused garlic-olive oil mixture. Stir 3 to 4 times until the flour has moistened. Let sit for another 5 minutes.

Stir in the remaining 1 1/2 cups of flour and the salt. Once the dough comes together, transfer to a floured board and knead the dough 10 to 15 times until smooth. Transfer to a large oiled bowl, cover with a warm, damp towel and let rise for 1 hour. (It’s best to let the dough rise in a warmer area of your kitchen).
After 1 hour, heat oven to 450°F (230°C). Use two tablespoons of the remaining garlic-olive oil mixture to oil a 9-inch by 13-inch rimmed baking sheet.

Transfer the dough to the baking sheet then press it down into the pan. Use your fingers to dimple the dough then drizzle the top with the remaining 2 tablespoons of the garlic-olive oil mixture. Let the dough rise for 20 minutes until it puffs slightly.
Bake until golden brown, 15 to 20 minutes. Cool baked focaccia bread on a wire rack.
notes: Don't skimp on the herbs. Do more that the recipe says if you like.

title: Pasta Salad
commentary: We introduced this recipe to our family BBQ nights, and it quickly became a part of the regular rotation.
instructions: Cook the pasta according to package instructions. Drain and rinse under cold water.
In a large bowl, combine the pasta, cherry tomatoes, cucumber, red onion, olives, and feta cheese.
In a small bowl, whisk together the olive oil, white wine vinegar, salt, and pepper.
Pour the dressing over the pasta salad and toss to combine.
Serve immediately or refrigerate for later.
notes: You can add other vegetables like peppers or zucchini.
This also works especially well with pearl couscous.
As an alternative to Mediterranean flavours, you can also do roasted vegetables.
Add a bit of stone fruit to mix it up a bit.

title: Lentil Pomegranate Salad
commentary: This recipe was sent to us by Kimmie's grand nonna. She altered the original recipe to have pomegranate seeds and candied walnuts (instead of regular walnuts).
ingredients: 1 tin (400g) cooked lentils, drained and rinsed
1/2 tsp cumin seeds, roasted and crushed
1/4 cup icing sugar (optional, if making candied walnuts)
instructions: (Optional) To make candied walnuts, cook walnuts in boiling water for 5 minutes, then drain well. Toss walnuts with icing sugar. Heat 1 tbsp of oil in a small frying pan over medium heat, add walnuts and cook for 2 minutes until golden. Remove from heat and let cool on parchment paper, season to taste.
Shake the olive oil, lemon juice, pomegranate molasses, and crushed cumin seeds together in a small jar to make the dressing.
In a large bowl, combine the cooked lentils, chopped walnuts, sliced spring onions, and chopped parsley.
Pour the dressing over the salad and toss to combine.
Just before serving, sprinkle the pomegranate seeds on top for a burst of color and flavour.
notes: This is a versatile recipe. We sometimes like to add feta cheese.

title: Oriental Coleslaw
commentary: This coleslaw is a staple at our Saturday night BBQs at Nan and Grandpa's house.

Dressing: 1 tbsp rice wine vinegar or wine vinegar
Salad: handful of coriander and/or mint leaves, roughly chopped

instructions: Measure all dressing ingredients into a screw-top jar and shake to combine.
Put the vegetables into a large bowl and toss to combine, adding just enough dressing to moisten everything (extra dressing will keep well in the fridge).
notes: Red cabbage works very well in this recipe.
attribution: New Zealand Barbeque Cookbook by Simon & Alison Holst

title: Sweet and sour yams
commentary: This was another regular side dish of Nan's when yams were in season.
ingredients: 400g yams, top and tailed, cut into 1-2 cm pieces
instructions: Place the chopped yams into a microwave dish with lid.
Put butter, sugar, and vinegar in microwave bowl with lid and melt on high for 30-60 seconds.
Add yams and mix together.
Microwave for 2 minutes and stir, then microwave again for 1-2 minutes until yams are tender.
Sprinkle with cornflour then stir in (add a little water or more vinegar if needed) and microwave a further 30-60 seconds so a sauce firms.

title: Leeks in white sauce
commentary: This was another classic side dish that we had growing up, often served with potatoes and meat.
instructions: Cut leeks up into 1cm rounds or semi-circles and cover with water. Bring to the boil and add 1/2 tsp of salt, then simmer for 5-10 minutes until cooked.
Drain water and add milk.
Mix cornflour with water to make a paste and stir into leeks and milk.
Return to stovetop and bring to the boil, stirring to prevent sticking whilst sauce thickens. Boil for 1-2 minutes. If sauce too thick add extra milk. Season to taste with salt and pepper.

title: Shredded cabbage with walnuts
commentary: This was a regular side dish that Nan made to go with meals that we had growing up.
instructions: Place shredded cabbage in a microwave dish with lid.
Add vinegar, sugar and butter.
Microwave on high for 1 minute, then stir or shake cabbage and cook again for 30 seconds to 1 minute. Cabbage should still be crisp (do not over cook). Serve straight away.

title: Marinated beetroot
commentary: This is another of Nan's classic recipes which is a regular feature of our family BBQs when beetroot from her garden are in season.
instructions: Wash beetroot and trim leaves, leaving 1-2 cm of stalk. Place in a saucepan and cover with water. Bring to the boil and simmer for 30-40 minutes until tender.
Drain and when cool enough to handle, peel the skin off with your fingers or a knife. Slice into rounds in a small-medium size serving bowl.
Sprinkle up to 1 tsp of sugar over the top. Next add 1-2 tbsp malt vinegar. Finally add water so that all beetroot is covered.
Taste to test. If too sweet, add more vinegar. If too vinegary, add more sugar and water.
notes: If you top and tail the beetroot then it will all bleed out when cooking.

title: Bread sauce
commentary: Grandma used to make bread sauce to go with roast chicken when we went around for dinner.
instructions: Stick the cloves into the onion and place in a saucepan with the bay leaf and milk. Heat gently until just below boiling point, then remove from heat and leave to infuse for 15 minutes.
Bring slowly to the boil, remove from the heat, cover and leave to infuse for 10-15 minutes. Remove onion and bay leaf.
Add butter and breadcrumbs to the milk in the pan. Add seasoning and simmer gently to reheat, stirring occasionally. Taste for seasoning before serving with chicken, turkey, or game bird.

title: Asparagus with vinegarette
commentary: We love asparagus season. This is one of the nice ways that we have it at a family BBQ.
instructions: Wash and trim asparagus and cook until tender. Immediately plunge into cold water and when cold drain and blot dry with paper towel.
Put dressing ingredients in a jar and shake well. Pour over asparagus.

title: American Pancakes
commentary: For some reason the Chelsea Sugar recipe is our go-to recipe for pancakes. Easy and delicious.
ingredients: ½ tsp finely grated lemon zest (optional)
instructions: Sift the flour and White Sugar into a bowl. Make a well in the centre of the dry ingredients.
Add the lemon zest, milk, egg and melted butter/margarine and whisk to combine.
Heat a large non-stick frying pan over medium low heat and grease lightly with butter or oil.
For each pancake, place 2 tablespoons of batter into the pan. Cook for 2 minutes or until bubbles form on the surface.
Turn and cook for a further 1 to 2 minutes or until the pancakes are cooked through.
Garnish with berries, bananas and maple syrup.

title: British Pancakes
commentary: These are the pancakes which I grew up with. Mum would occasionally make them as a weekend treat. However, Kimmie grew up with the more fluffier style pancakes. We had a long dispute over what constitues a pancake, so we settled for calling one style British and the other style American.
instructions: Sift the flour and salt, make a well in the middle and whisk in the egg. Whisk in the milk, then leave to stand for 20 minutes.
Heat a pan and coat lightly with oil or butter. Add a ladleful of batter and tilt the pan to coat thinly.
Fry pancake for 1½-2 minutes until golden, carefully turning once.
notes: Rest pancake batter for 20 minutes and you'll get a lighter pancake.
Always weigh out your ingredients. Too much flour and not enough milk will result in a dense pancake.
When frying, use an equal mix of butter and oil - using just enough to coat the bottom of the pan. The butter helps with flavour and the oil stops the butter from burning.
Flip your pancake when the edges look set and dry.
And only flip your pancake once - otherwise it will be tough.
Don't worry if the first pancake doesn't come out right - use it to test the temperature of the pan and adjust accordingly.

title: Corn Fritters
commentary: I like this recipe because it doesn't use eggs, and it has lots of chunky corn in it.
ingredients: 1/4 cup chopped fresh herbs (parsley, coriander, etc.)
instructions: In a large bowl, combine the corn, lime zest, lime juice, and herbs. Mix until well combined.
Add the flour and salt, and give a stir, then add water and mix until a thick batter forms. Leave for a couple of minutes.
Heat a non-stick frying pan over medium heat and add a little oil.
Spoon tablespoons of the mixture into the pan, flattening them slightly.
Cook for 4 minutes on each side, or until golden brown.
notes: Serve with sour cream, sweet chilli sauce, or avocado.
Alternatively, HP sauce is also good.
attribution: adapted from Hello Fresh: Recipes that Work

title: Waffles
cook_time: 1 hour (if making one at a time in a small waffle maker)
commentary: Another great weekend breakfast! We like to serve with maple syrup, berries, and yoghurt.
instructions: Beat the eggs in a mixer until well mixed.
Add remaining ingredients and mix until smooth.
Leave the mixture for at least half an hour.
Preheat the waffle maker.
Use a pastry brush to lightly grease the non-stick coating of the baking plates with oil or butter.
Pour mixture into waffle maker.
Cook until golden brown.
notes: This makes a lot. We halve it (but do 3 eggs), and it still feeds all of us for breakfast.
This recipe is for the Anko mini waffle maker.
attribution: Anko Waffle Maker FS-315-W User Manual

title: Porridge
commentary: This has been one of my go-to breakfasts for a long time.
instructions: Mix the oats and milk in a microwave-safe bowl.
Microwave on high for 90 seconds.
Stir in the greek yoghurt.
Top with maple syrup and fresh or stewed fruit if desired.

title: Toasties
commentary: A lunchtime staple from my student days. Chilli bean toasties were one of my favourite lunches.

Chilli bean toastie: 2 tbsp chilli beans (Watties brand works best)

instructions: Butter one side of each slice of bread.
Place one slice buttered-side down on sandwich press.
Add cheese and desired filling.
Top with the other slice, buttered-side up.
Lower top of sandwich press and cook until golden brown and cheese is melted.

title: Self-saucing Chocolate Pudding
commentary: Growing up, Aunty Barbara's self-saucing chocolate pudding was a family favourite. This recipe is very similar, but with less sugar and more cocoa powder!

Pudding: Vanilla ice cream or cream, to serve

instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C bake (160°C fan-forced). Grease a 6-cup capacity ovenproof baking dish.
Sift self raising flour and cocoa into a large bowl. Stir in the brown sugar.
Whisk butter, milk and egg in a jug. Slowly add to flour mixture, stirring until well combined and smooth. Spoon into baking dish and smooth the top.

For the sauce combine the brown sugar and cocoa and sprinkle over the pudding. Slowly pour the boiling water over the back of a large metal spoon to cover pudding. Place dish onto a baking tray and bake for 35 to 40 minutes or until pudding bounces back when pressed gently in the centre.
Serve hot with vanilla ice cream or cream.
notes: A dry self saucing pudding usually means it was overbaked or didn't have enough boiling water added. To keep it saucy, measure liquids carefully and bake until the sponge is just set. Don't overcook!
You can prepare the batter and topping ahead of time, but it's best to bake self saucing pudding just before serving. Add the hot water and bake fresh so the sauce stays warm and gooey.

title: Tiramisu
commentary: I often make this for Christmas. It's one of Kimmie's favourites and is always a hit.
ingredients: 6 Tbsp coffee liqueur (e.g. Kahlua or Tia Maria)
24 large sponge fingers or savoiardi biscuits
instructions: Prepare a double boiler by bringing a few centimetres of water to a simmer in a saucepan. Place a glass or stainless steel bowl over the top, so that it fits snugly without touching the water below.
