Place egg yolks, caster sugar and vanilla in the bowl. Whisk until thick, pale and creamy (3-4 minutes). Remove from heat.
Place the mascarpone and cream in a separate large bowl. Using an electric mixer, whip to soft peaks. Fold into the egg mixture.

Mix the coffee with the coffee liqueur. Dunk the sponge fingers quickly in the coffee mixture, making sure they are completely immersed. Don't leave them in for more than a second or two as they can turn soggy.
Layer one third of the sponge fingers in a serving dish (approximately 24cm x 16cm) and top with a third of the mascarpone cream. Repeat twice to form three layers.
Sift cocoa evenly over the top. Cover and chill for at least 3 hours before serving.
notes: To save time you can substitute the egg yolk mixture with 1 cup (250g) of store-bought custard.
Blend the cooled egg yolk mixture into the mascarpone before you fold in the whipped cream and it will come out perfectly fluffy

title: Crumble
commentary: This is one of our classic BBQ night desserts for when there is lots of fruit to be used up.
instructions: Preheat the oven to 180°C (160°C fan). Put the flour into a bowl. Add the butter and rub in with your fingertips until the mixture resembles fine bread-crumbs. Stir in half the sugar. Put aside.
Arrange half the apples in a 1.1 litre pie dish and sprinkle with the rest of the sugar. Add the remaining apple slices to the dish.
Spoon the crumble mixture over the fruit. Bake in the oven for about 45 mins or until the fruit is soft. Serve hot with custard or a drizzle of double cream.
notes: Serve with vanilla ice-crema or cream.
Use whatever fruit is available. Our common flavours are apple, apple & rhubarb, feijoa, plum, or apple & blackberry.
Add oats and/or nuts to the crumble topping for extra texture and flavour.

title: Carrot Cake
commentary: Grand Nonna has made this cake many times - always successful and delicious.

Cake: Combine wholemeal self-raising flour, plain flour, bicarbonate of soda, cinnamon and nutmeg in a large bowl.
Add brown sugar and oil to the flour mixture and stir well.
Beat eggs with heavy sour cream until combined.
Add egg mixture and grated carrot to the flour mixture and fold until just combined.
Pour mixture into a lined 20cm square baking tin.
Beat together icing ingredients, and spread over cooled cake.
Icing: Beat together unsalted butter, cream cheese, icing sugar and grated lemon rind until smooth and fluffy.
Spread icing over the cooled cake.

notes: This recipe has been transcribed from shorthand notes
Use unsalted butter in the icing
The icing is essential to this recipe!

title: Walnut Syrup Cake
commentary: Like the Carrot Cake, Grand Nonna has also made this cake many times - also always successful and delicious.

Cake: Cream butter, sugar and orange rind together until light and fluffy.
Add egg yolks and beat well.
Sift flour, semolina, baking powder and cinnamon together twice.
Fold the dry ingredients into the creamed mixture alternately with milk.
Gently mix in the ground walnuts.
Beat egg whites until stiff peaks form.
Fold the egg whites into the batter using a metal spoon.
Pour mixture into a greased and lined tin (33 x 23 x 5 cm).
Bake in moderate oven for 45 minutes or until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean.
Syrup: While cake is baking, place sugar, water, cloves, cinnamon stick, lemon juice and lemon rind in a heavy saucepan.
Stir over heat until sugar dissolves.
Bring to the boil and simmer over moderate heat for 10 minutes.
Pour the strained boiling syrup over the hot cake immediately after removing from oven.
Serving: When cold, cut into squares or diamonds and lift out of tin.
Serve with whipped cream if liked.

notes: Make the syrup while the cake is baking so it's ready to pour over the hot cake
Use a metal spoon when folding in the egg whites to preserve air
Pour the hot syrup over the hot cake for best absorption
The tin size is 33 x 23 x 5 cm (13 x 9 x 2 in)

title: Flourless Chocolate and Roasted Pecan Cake
commentary: This is the cake recipe which Nonna often uses for special occasions. Very decadent and rich!

Cake: Icing sugar (optional) for dusting or Ganache (see recipe below)
Lightly sweetened double thick cream, to serve
Ganache (shiny chocolate icing): 250g good-quality dark chocolate, broken into small chunks

Cake: Preheat your oven to 180C. Butter a 23cm springform tin and line the base with buttered baking paper. Dust the tin with flour (or rice flour if you're avoiding wheat completely), and tap out the excess. Set it aside.

Melt the butter in a medium-sized saucepan over low heat. Add the chocolate and whisk it in until it's melted too. Take the pan off the heat, then add the cocoa, stirring it in until the mixture is thick and smooth. Set it aside to cool a little.

Meanwhile, whiz the pecans in a food processor to chop them as finely as possible. Keep an eye on them as you do this, stopping the machine every so often to check them so they don’t become oily and form a paste. Set them aside.

In a large bowl, whisk the eggs and sugar with a balloon whisk until they're just blended together, then whisk in the warm chocolate mixture until it's well combined. Mix in the brandy and vanilla extract. Finally, stir in the ground pecans – at this stage the batter looks very loose and sloppy but that’s just how it should be.

Pour the batter into the prepared tin. Pop it in the oven and bake the cake for 40-45 minutes or until the sides are set but the middle 15cm or so of the cake is still a bit wobbly when you gently shake the tin.

Remove it to a wire rack and leave the cake to cool completely in the tin. Once cool, release the sides of the tin and carefully invert the cake onto a plate lined with baking paper. Cover the cake with plastic wrap, and chill it in the fridge, where it keeps well for at least a week.
When you're ready to eat it, slide the cake off the baking paper onto a serving dish then dust it with icing sugar. Serve it with cream.
Ganache (shiny chocolate icing): Makes about 1 3/4 cups - enough to ice 2 medium-sized cakes

Ganache is lovely stuff and terribly easy to make. There are lots of different versions of it (many use cream rather than butter), but basically whatever recipe you use, you end up with a silky, chocolate glaze that forms a glossy icing when it’s poured over a cake.

Put the chocolate, butter and water into a medium-sized, heavy-based saucepan over low heat. Let the chocolate and butter melt, stirring the mixture frequently, until the ganache is smooth. (I use a small, flat sauce whisk for this, as it gets right into the corners of the pan where the chocolate tends to clump a bit.) The most important thing to keep in mind when you’re making ganache is that it mustn’t get too hot and boil – if it does, it becomes oily and grainy and there’s not much chance of salvaging it, I’m afraid. Once it’s silky smooth, take it off the heat and let it cool until it’s barely warm and of a thick pouring consistency. Pour as much as you need evenly over the cake so it trickles down the sides. Leave the ganache to set

Scrape any leftover ganache into an airtight container, seal it tightly and store it in the fridge. When you're ready to use it, gently warm it over very low heat, then let it cool to the right consistency. It’s wonderful to have on hand when you want to dress up a cake in a hurry, as all you need do is swoosh some ganache over it and it immediately looks chic and special.

notes: Dutch-processed cocoa removes some of the cocoa's acidity, giving it a softer, smoother flavour, richer colour, and tends to dissolve more easily. Usually available in good delicatessens and specialty food shops.
This is a dense, rich and very chocolate-y cake that dissolves on your tongue, leaving a hint of dark, bitter-sweet warmth.

Ganache sets beautifully most of the time, but like all chocolate, it's a bit temperamental in hot, humid weather. If it won't set and is still sticky, put the ganache-coated cake, uncovered, in the fridge for a short time so the quick burst of chilled air sets it.

In hot, humid weather, store the cake in the fridge. Put the cake uncovered in the fridge until the ganache has set, then cover it in plastic wrap. Before serving, remove the wrap and return the cake to cool room temperature so the ganache regains its sheen.
Ganache makes about 1 3/4 cups - enough to ice 2 medium-sized cakes.

title: South African Milk Tart
commentary: One of Jase's favourite desserts
instructions: Prepare a 25cm pie dish with grease spray.
For the smear crust: Cream 100g softened salted butter and 100g granulated sugar until light and fluffy, about 3 minutes.
Add 2 eggs one at a time, mixing well after each addition. Fold in 100g plain flour until the mixture is well combined.

Using your fingers, smear a very thin, even layer of the mixture over the base and sides of the prepared pie dish. Use a light touch so the mixture distributes evenly; this forms a delicate thin crust. If you have extra mixture, freeze for later use.
For the filling: Preheat the oven to 160°C (fan or conventional equivalent). Put 600ml milk into a pot and warm over medium-low heat until scalding (just below boiling).
In a separate bowl, whisk together 150ml milk, 150g granulated sugar, 50g plain flour, 50g cornflour and 2g salt into a smooth, lump-free paste.

While stirring, gradually add the hot scalded milk to the paste to temper it, then return the combined mixture to the pot. Heat over medium-low, stirring continuously, until the mixture reaches its first boil and forms a thick plop (bubbles).

Transfer the hot milk mixture to a mixing bowl. Lightly whisk the 4 egg yolks and whisk them into the warm milk mixture. Add 100g salted butter and 10ml vanilla essence and stir until combined; the butter will melt into the warm custard. Set aside to cool slightly.
In a clean bowl, whip the 4 egg whites to stiff peaks (but not dry).
Once the milk mixture has cooled to warm (not hot), fold the whipped egg whites gently into the milk mixture until just combined.
Pour the filling gently into the prepared smear crust, smoothing the top. Sprinkle generously with ground cinnamon.
Bake in the preheated oven for 30 minutes, or until the top is set and slightly golden.
Serve warm from the oven or chilled from the fridge (many prefer chilled).
notes: All eggs used in this recipe are Size 7 (large).
The smear crust recipe may give extra; leftover crust freezes well.
Store milk tart in an airtight container in the fridge for up to 5 days.
attribution: Regal Shortbread Co. in the Pandemic Pack Wellington cookbook

title: Tuscan Apple Cake
commentary: A classic cake which we would have when we were tired of chocolate cakes!
ingredients: 4 large granny smith or ballarat cooking apples (about 800g)
2 extra large or 3 small eggs, room temperature
instructions: Preheat oven to 190°C. Prepare 25cm non-stick round spring form pan. Line with greaseproof paper, butter and flour (I just do the base).
Peel, core, and quarter appled, slice thinly by hand or use food processor attachment.
In a small bowl vcombine flour, baking powder, and salt.
PLace eggs, sugar, and vanilla extract in a large bowl and whisk until sugar dissolved and the mixture is pale and thick.
Fold the flour into the egg mixture with a wooden spoon, the fold in the milk, then the butter and finally apple slices.
Pour the mixture into the prepared pan (sit on baking tray), pack apples in and flatten the surface with wooden spoon.
Bake 10 minutes on low shelf of oven, then transfer to middle rack and continue to bake for 50-55 minutes until golden and toothpick inserted in centre comes out clean.
Remove from oven and run knife around edge of pan to loosen. Leave in pan until cool. Unmold cake and transfer to platter. Dust with icing sugar to serve.

title: Chocolate Roulade with Raspberries
commentary: Another one of our regular desserts. This one works well around Christmas time when we have fresh raspberries from Nan's garden.

Roulade: Grease a 30.5 x 20.5cm swiss roll tin. Line with greaseproof paper and grease the paper. Dust with caster sugar and flour.

Whisk the eggs and caster sugar in a bowl placed over a pan of hot water (just simemring on stove top) until pale, creamy and thich enough to lave a trail on surface. Sift in the cocoa powder and gently fold in.
Turn mixture into tin and bank at 200°C for 10-12 minutes.
Meanwhile, please a sheet of greaseproof paper over a damp tea towel. Dredge paper with caster sugar.
Quickly turn the cake out onto paper, trim edges, and roll up with paper inside. Leave to cool on wire rack.
When cake is cold, unroll and remove paper. Spread with whipped cream and fresh berries. Roll up and dredge with icing sugar or chocolate fudge icing.
Chocolate fudge icing (optional): Melt butter in microwave, add sifted cocoa and icing sugar.
Reheat and add 1-2 tbsp of water to make runny icing. Pour over roulade to set.

notes: If icing is too runn, add extra icing sugar as needed.

title: Chocolate upside-down raspberry cake
commentary: Nan grows fresh raspberries. This is one of her recipes which uses them in a delicious cake.
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C. Grease 22cm round spring form tin.
Make topping by placking ingredients (chocolate, raspberry jam, and cream) into pan or microwave jug, heat gently until melted and smooth then pour into prepared tin.
In a large bowl cream the butter and sugar until pale and fluffy, then add eggs one at a time, beating in well after each.
In a bowl, mix cocoa powder with 125ml hot water, stirring until smooth, then add milk, vanilla, and jam. Whisk to combine.
Sift flour, baking powder,and salt into another bowl.
Using a large spoon, fold dry ingredients and the cocoa mix into the creamed mixture, alternating the two, then fold in raspberries.
Pour mixture into tin and smooth over. Bake for 30-35 minutes, Leave cake to cool in tin for 15 minutes, before turning cake out onto plate.

title: Coffee and Walnut Loaf Cake
commentary: Nan's favourite type of cake is coffee cake as was her dad's. Her mum used to make a bit of an opera cake with two sponges and a layer of icing in the middle and top.

Cake: 1 tbsp instant coffee dissolved in 1 tbsp warm water
Buttercream: 1 tbsp instant coffee dissolved in 1 tbsp warm water

instructions: Preheat oven to 175C (conventional).
Cream the butter and sugar until light and fluffy.
Then add the coffee essence and mix until well distributed.
Add the eggs, gradually, beating well between each addition.
Fold in the flour and then the walnuts.
Spoon the mixture into a lined 1kg loaf tin and bake for approximately 45 minutes, or until a skewer comes out clean.
Remove from the oven and let cool briefly before lifting out onto a wire rack to cool.
For the buttercream, cream the butter and sugar until light and fluffy, then add the milk and coffee. Spread over the top of the cake and scatter with walnuts.
Slice and enjoy!
notes: I used a shot of espresso for the coffee in thie recipe and that worked great.
Nan prefers to have plainer icing without the butter (just icing sugar, coffee, and water glaze).

title: Cheese Scones
commentary: When we lived in London I felt homesick for cheese scones, so I researched all of the Wellington cheese scone recipes that I could find. This recipe is the result of that research and combining the best bits of each.
instructions: Put all the dry ingredients into a large bowl, including the cheese and mix well.
Create a well in the dry ingredients and pour the milk in. Combine mixture with a knife (remember to fold it in) but do not over work it.

Wet your hands and roll into good even sized balls which can be weighed if desired or done by sight. The most important thing to remember is to be light with the rolling, quick with hands and even in size.
Once the mix is combined, place the rolled balls onto an oven tray lined with baking paper and top the scones with more grated cheese.
Bake at 220°C for 10-15 mins or until golden in colour.
Let scones rest for five minutes before serving.
notes: Make sure your hands are wet when rolling the scones. Run your hands under the tap every second scone.
Tasty cheese works well for this recipe, but you can also use up any cheese you have on hand.
Unlikely many recipes, this recipe actually works best if you use pre-grated cheese.
Smoked paprika can be used instead of cayenne pepper.
Chopped herbs can be added for extra flavour, such as parsley or chives.
attribution: This is a combination of the Ministry of Food and Pravda cheese scone recipes.

title: Sourdough Bread
commentary: In early 2020 I started experimenting with making my own sourdough bread. This was fortunate timing, as it was right before covid-19 lockdowns started and so at least we had plenty of bread through the lockdowns.
instructions: If storing starter in fridge, remove from fridge and leave at room temperature overnight to allow to bubble up.
Feed starter, and add excess starter to mixing bowl.
Add both flour measures and salt to mixing bowl and add first measure of 350ml water. Mix well then allow to rest for at least 10 minutes.
Add second measure of 25ml water and fold dough in bowl. It should have a slightly slimy feel.

Every couple of hours in the mixing bowl fold the dough over itself from all four corners. Do this until the dough had puffed up a bit. The time this takes will vary depending on the ambient temperature.
Oil a bread pan with olive oil and then turn dough out into the pan. Allow to rise until it's about at the top of the pan.
Bake in the oven at 200 fan-forced for 25 minutes. When finished turn out onto a rack for bread to cool. Don't leave it in the pan or it will get soggy.
attribution: Adapted from Tartine Bread by Chad Robertson

title: Sourdough Starter (feeding)
instructions: Remove starter from jar, leaving about 2 tsp behind, and use it to either bake or discard.
Add both measures of flour and water to jar containing the remaining 2 tsp of starter, and mix well so that all flour is dissolved.
Store in fridge for up to a fortnight before feeding again.
attribution: Adapted from Tartine Bread by Chad Robertson

title: No Knead Bread
commentary: For when not making sourdough, this is my favourite bread recipe. It tastes great for a non-sourdough recipe.
instructions: Mix the flour, yeast, and salt in a bowl, then add the water and stir. The dough should be very sticky.
Cover the bowl with plastic wrap and let it rise at room temperature for 3 hours.
After 3 hours, the dough should be bubbly and doubled in size.
Preheat the oven to 220°C fan-forced and place a Dutch oven (with lid) inside to heat up.
Turn the dough out onto a floured surface and fold in each of the sides, then flip upside down onto a sheet of baking paper.
Carefully remove the hot Dutch oven from the oven and place the dough inside.
Cover with the lid and bake for 30 minutes.
Remove the lid and bake for an additional 15 minutes, until the bread is golden brown.
Let cool before slicing.
notes: To be honest, I don't bother preheating the Dutch oven. I just put the dough in and it works fine.
If using normal bread yeast, dissolve the yeast in the water first.

title: Feijoa Chutney
commentary: This is the recipe which mum uses to make feijoa chutney. It's one of my favourites to eat on toast with swiss cheese. Kimmie hates it!
instructions: Place all ingredients in large pot and bring to a boil.
Reduce heat and simmer, stirring occasionally towards the end, until mixture is thick and brown (up to 3 hours).
Bottle into sterile jars while warm.
Keeps for months.
Refrigerate once open.
notes: A great way to use up excess feijoas!

Some feijoa varieties are much sweeter and juicier than others, so your chutney may vary from one batch to the next. We have reduced the vinegar in this recipe from 1 litre to 750ml, but you may wish to try it after it has cooked for an hour and adjust the flavour balance to suit your taste.
The cooking time will vary depending on the juiciness of your feijoas and the size of your pot. If you are making a double batch it will take even longer to reduce.

title: Greek Yoghurt (EasiYo)
commentary: Economical, thick, and creamy Greek yoghurt using EasiYo base and extra milk powder. No straining, no thickeners, no preservatives.
ingredients: 3 tbsp greek yoghurt (or 3 tbsp of plain EasiYo Greek yoghurt sachet)
instructions: Gather all ingredients and boil the kettle.
In the yoghurt making canister, fill to the halfway mark with cold water. Add the yoghurt starter.
Add the milk powder.
Place the lid on and shake vigorously until the milk powder is dissolved. Remove the lid and fill to the fill line with more water. Screw the lid on tightly and shake again.
Fill the EasiYo yoghurt maker to the fill line with boiling water.
Place the canister inside and screw on the yoghurt maker lid. Leave for 24 hours. Remove and refrigerate. Keeps for seven days.
notes: EasiYo real base and culture is intended for you to use the entire sachet for a batch of yoghurt but using just 2-3 tablespoons is enough if you add extra milk powder. This makes it a lot cheaper.
Store the remainder of the opened base sachet in an airtight container in the refrigerator.
You can use 3 tablespoons from the previous batch of yoghurt as a starter for your next batch.
For sweetened yoghurt, add 1-2 tablespoons of sugar to the canister when mixing in the milk powder and starter.
Some people are happy with yoghurt cultured for 12-14 hours, but 24 hours gives a thick and creamy consistency.
If you don't have an EasiYo, you can use a slow cooker: preheat on low for 30 minutes, turn off, fill with boiling water, place your sealed container with the mixture inside, and incubate as above.
Adjust the amount of milk powder to get your desired thickness. More milk powder = thicker yoghurt.

title: Garlic Butter Flatbreads
commentary: A handy recipe for flat breads to save buying them from the shops. We eat these with curry or as a wrap for koftas.

Flatbreads: 2 cups self-raising flour (or plain/high grade flour + 2 1/2 tsp baking powder)
3/4 cup milk (or plain yoghurt or water)

instructions: Place flour, milk, oil and salt in a bowl and mix to combine.
Knead on a lightly floured surface for about 5 minutes until smooth and elastic.
Divide the dough into 4 or 6 portions and rest on the bench for at least 15-30 minutes (longer is fine; wrap if resting overnight).
If making garlic & herb butter, mix softened butter, chopped garlic and parsley together and set aside.
Heat a large heavy-based skillet or frypan over medium heat. Roll each piece of dough into a roughly 25cm circle and lightly brush with oil.
Place the dough in the hot pan and cook about 2 minutes per side until puffed and golden. Repeat with remaining pieces.
Brush with garlic herb butter while still hot, if using, and serve immediately.
attribution: Nadia's Comfort Kitchen by Nadia Lim

title: Lemon Poppy Seed Muffins
commentary: One of Kimmie's party tricks. We often make this recipe.

Muffins: zest of 2 small lemons (or 1 large lemon, about 2 tablespoons)
3/4 cup plain fat free Greek yogurt

instructions: Preheat oven to 190°C and grease a muffin pan or line with cupcake liners.
In a medium bowl whisk together flour, baking soda, and salt.
In a large bowl cream together butter, sugar, eggs, vanilla extract, lemon zest and juice, and Greek yogurt.
Add dry ingredients to wet ingredients and mix until combined. Stir in poppyseeds.
Fill muffin tins about 2/3 full with muffin batter. Bake for 12-16 minutes until an inserted toothpick comes out clean. Allow to cool on a wire rack.

To make the icing, cream softened butter and cream cheese for 2 minutes until very fluffy. Add powdered sugar and lemon juice and mix well. Taste and add more lemon juice if desired. Drizzle icing over cooled muffins. (If icing is too thick you can warm it up in the microwave for a few seconds.)
Serve immediately or store at room temperature in an airtight container for up to 3-5 days.
notes: Note that we don't tend to make the icing for this recipe. We usually just do a glaze with icing sugar and lemon juice.
If you are going to make this icing, you should halve it, as it makes a lot.

title: Big chunky kiwi chocolate biscuits
commentary: The biscuits were a regular lunchbox morning tea snack.
instructions: Preheat oven to 170°C.
Cream butter, sugar, and condensed milk.
Add flour and baking powder and a pinch of salt.
Break chocolate into small chunks and add to mixture. Combine together, then take a spoonful of the mixture and roll into a ball, flatten onto a baking tray lined with greaseproof paper.
Bake at 170°C for 12-15 minutes. Cool on wire rack.
notes: Use a fork to flatten the balls for a wavy mark on the top.

title: Christmas Cookies
commentary: We made these cookies for Lexie's Playcentre graduation and they were a hit with the kids.

Royal Icing: 1 tsp lemon juice (strained to remove any pulp)

Cookies: Using an electric mixer, beat butter and Chelsea White Sugar together until pale and creamy, scraping down the sides of the bowl occasionally.
Beat in vanilla and salt. Add egg and beat well.
Slowly add the flour and mix until just combined.
Place some cling film on the bench and tip the mixture into this. Slightly flatten into an oval shape. Wrap up well and refrigerate for at least two hours.
Preheat oven to 160°C and line two baking trays with baking paper.

Split the dough into two portions to make it easier to work with. Knead the dough gently using well-floured hands, then on a well-floured bench, roll the dough to the desired thickness (5mm works well).
Use cookie cutters to cut out shapes and carefully transfer to prepared trays.
Continue to create shapes, kneading the dough scraps back together, until all dough is used.
Bake for 13-15 minutes, until pale golden. Keep an eye on them from about 12 minutes, to ensure they don't brown too much.
Cool on trays for 3 minutes, then transfer to a wire rack to cool completely.
Royal Icing: Place the egg whites and lemon juice in a large mixing bowl and stir to combine.

Add the Chelsea Icing Sugar one cup at a time, mixing well in between with electric beaters, until fully incorporated. Add the last ½ cup a little at a time, until you reach soft peaks (you may not need to use all of it).

You can test for the correct consistency of icing by dragging a knife over the surface. If the surface smoothes over in approximately 10 seconds, the icing is at the right consistency. Mix in water in very small quantities, until this consistency is achieved.
Split the icing into small bowls if you wish to make different colours. Add food colour if desired (gel colours work best).
Spoon into ziplock plastic bags or piping bags fitted with small round piping tips, ready for icing.

notes: Alternatively use a simple icing sugar and milk icing instead.

title: Banana Oat Pikelets
commentary: This is a nice no-added-sugar snack that we often make for the kids.
ingredients: 1 cup milk (240ml) - you may or may not need the full cup
instructions: Whisk egg
Add dry ingredients (flour, oats, baking powder, cinnamon), whisk/mix
Add half of the milk, mix
Add mashed banana, mix to combine
If needed, add more milk and continue to add milk until you have a thick pancake-like batter
Heat a pan and grease with a small amount of oil (butter, rice bran oil, coconut oil, etc)
Drop in spoonfuls of the batter. Cook until small bubbles pop on the surface, flip over and cook the other side
Serve

title: Flapjacks
commentary: These were one of Nan's favourite treats when she was young, and the featured regularly in our school lunchboxes.
instructions: Grease shallow 18cm square cake tin and line with grease-proof paper.
Melt the butter with the sugar and syrup and pour in the rolled oats. Mix well and turn mixture into prepared tin and press down well.
Bake in oven at 180°C for about 20 minutes, until golden brown. Cool slightly in tin, mark into fingers with a sharp knife and loosen around edges of tin.
When firm, remove from tin and cool on wire rack, the break into fingers. The flapjacks can be stored in an air tight container for up to 1 week.

title: Chocolate Caramel Slice
commentary: Another lunchbox regular growing up.
instructions: Preheat oven to 180°C and line a 20cm square bakign tin with baking paper (shallow tin).
Melt butter, syrup, and brown sugar in a saucepan large enough to contain all ingredients. Mix in flour, baking powder, and rolled oats until combined. Press into prepared tin. Bake for 15 minutes.
For topping, melt all ingredients except chocolate in a saucepan and slowly boil for 7 minutes, stirring all the time. Pour over cooked base.
Melt chocolate and pour over the top. Leave to cool and mark into squares just as chocolate is setting.
attribution: Nan's friend Alison from battle hill (appears to be adapted from Edmonds Cookbook)

title: Yorkshire Puddings
commentary: Classic British side, crisp and hollow, perfect for roast dinners.
ingredients: 100ml beef drippings, lard, shortening, or vegetable oil
instructions: Combine eggs, flour, milk, water, and salt in a medium bowl and whisk until a smooth batter is formed. Let batter rest at room temperature for at least 30 minutes. For best results, refrigerate batter overnight or up to 3 days. Remove from refrigerator while preheating oven.

Adjust oven rack to centre position and preheat oven to 230°C. Divide drippings (or other fat) evenly between two 8-inch cast iron or oven-safe non-stick skillets, two 6-well popover tins, one 12-well standard muffin tin, or one 24-well mini muffin tin. Preheat in oven until fat is smoking hot, about 10 minutes.

Transfer pans or tins to a heat-proof surface and divide batter evenly between every well (or between pans if using pans). Wells should be filled between 1/2 and 3/4 of the way (or 1/4 for pans). Immediately return to oven. Bake until puddings have quadrupled in volume, are deep brown, crisp, and sound hollow when tapped. Smaller ones take about 15 minutes, popover/skillet-sized about 25 minutes.
Serve immediately.
notes: Resting the batter overnight improves results.
Can use beef drippings, lard, shortening, or vegetable oil.
For best rise, ensure fat is smoking hot before adding batter.
